era_context: The early 11th century CE is marked by the consolidation of feudal structures in Western Europe, the Fatimid Caliphate's dominance in the Mediterranean, the Song Dynasty's cultural and technological peak in China, and the expansion of Islamic and Turkic powers across Central Asia and the Near East. This is a period of significant religious, political, and cultural transformation across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Scandinavia (sparse monastic records)

events.title: Sweyn I of Denmark's death and succession crisis
events.description: Sweyn I Forkbeard, King of Denmark and conqueror of England, died in 1014 or 1015 CE. His death triggered a succession crisis in Scandinavia and England. Sweyn's son Cnut the Great eventually succeeded him, but the transition was contested. Sweyn's death marked the end of an era of Scandinavian expansion and the beginning of a new phase of North Sea politics. The succession crisis had significant consequences for the political development of England, Denmark, and Norway.
key_figures: Sweyn I Forkbeard (King of Denmark, d. 1014 or 1015 CE)
Cnut the Great (Sweyn's son, eventual successor)
Harald II (Sweyn's other son, contested succession)
events.certainty_note: Sweyn I's death is traditionally dated to 1014 CE, but some sources suggest 1015 CE. The exact date is uncertain, with a margin of error of ±1 year.

events.title: Basil II's campaigns in the Balkans
events.description: Emperor Basil II (r. 976–1025 CE) continued his military campaigns against the Bulgarian Empire in 1015 CE. These campaigns were part of a systematic effort to consolidate Byzantine control over the Balkans. Basil II's campaigns were marked by military efficiency and the use of advanced siege warfare techniques. By 1015, the final phase of the conquest was approaching, with Bulgarian resistance being systematically overcome.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Basil II's reign

events.certainty_note: Basil II's campaigns in the Balkans are well-documented in Byzantine sources. By 1015, the final phase of conquest was underway.

events.title: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah's religious policies and the destruction of churches
events.region: Fatimid Caliphate, Egypt and the Levant
events.description: Caliph al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah (r. 996–1021 CE) continued his controversial religious policies in 1015 CE, including restrictions on Christian and Jewish communities and the destruction of churches and synagogues. The destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, which began in 1009 CE, was ongoing during this period. These policies were motivated by religious intolerance, political instability, and economic factors. The policies had significant consequences for Christian and Jewish communities in the Fatimid realm.

sources.name: Contemporary Islamic and Christian sources, including accounts by Yahya of Antioch

events.certainty_note: Al-Hakim's religious policies are well-attested in contemporary and later sources.

events.title: Song Dynasty's technological innovations and cultural achievements
events.description: The Northern Song Dynasty (960–1127 CE) continued to achieve significant technological and cultural advances in 1015 CE. Song China had developed advanced printing technology, including woodblock printing and movable type, which facilitated the spread of knowledge and literature. The Song period saw significant advances in mathematics, astronomy, medicine, and engineering. The Song court was a center of artistic and literary patronage. The Song economy was one of the most advanced in the world at this time.

sources.name: Song Dynasty official histories and records (Songshi)
Archaeological evidence: artifacts, manuscripts, and technological remains

events.certainty_note: The Song Dynasty's technological and cultural achievements are well-documented in contemporary and later sources, as well as archaeological evidence.

events.title: Rajendra I's early reign and military campaigns
events.description: Rajendra I (r. 1014–1044 CE) began his reign in 1014 CE and by 1015 CE was already engaged in military campaigns to consolidate and expand Chola power. Rajendra I was one of the greatest Chola emperors, known for his military campaigns, administrative reforms, and cultural patronage. His early campaigns were directed at consolidating control over South India and establishing naval dominance in the Indian Ocean.
key_figures: Rajaraja I (Rajendra I's father, previous emperor)
events.certainty_note: Rajendra I's accession and early campaigns are well-attested in inscriptions and later sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Sweyn I's death is traditionally given as 1014 CE, but some sources suggest 1015 CE. The exact date is uncertain. Additionally, the succession crisis following Sweyn I's death is not fully documented in contemporary sources, and later chronicles provide conflicting accounts of the transition to Cnut's rule.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1015 CE is moderate in quality, with significant regional variation. English and Scandinavian sources are relatively rich for the succession crisis following Sweyn I's death. Byzantine sources are detailed for the Balkans. Islamic sources provide information about the Fatimid Caliphate. Chinese sources are detailed for the Song Dynasty. Indian sources are sparse, relying heavily on inscriptions and later compilations. The surviving sources are biased toward elite political and military history. The experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups are largely absent from the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Sweyn I's death in 1014/1015 led to the succession of Cnut the Great and his conquest of England in 1016
Basil II's campaigns in 1015 continued and culminated in the final conquest of Bulgaria in 1018
Al-Hakim's religious policies in 1015 continued and intensified, leading to the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in 1018
Rajendra I's early campaigns in 1015 led to his continued expansion and naval dominance in the Indian Ocean

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
